meta.title: Technical Specifications | Quantum Shield
meta.description: Quantum Shield technical specifications - architecture, cryptographic algorithms, sequences, and parameters

title: Technical Specifications
description: Detailed specifications of Quantum Shield's architecture, quantum-resistant cryptography, processing flows, and system parameters.

architecture.title: Architecture
architecture.description: Quantum Shield consists of a 3-layer structure: L1 (Ethereum), L3 (Prover Network), and User Layer. Without using L2, our decentralized L3 network operated by Provers implements BFT consensus and quantum-resistant signatures.

l1.title: L1: Ethereum (Settlement Layer)
l1.description: The foundation layer ensuring final asset settlement and security enforcement
components: SPHINCSVerifier: On-chain verification of Prover signatures

l3.title: L3: Prover Network (Verification Layer)
l3.description: Decentralized network of L3 nodes operated by Provers. Handles both BFT consensus and SPHINCS+ signatures
components: Transparent recording of all state changes

user.title: User Layer (Self-Custody)
user.description: Self-custody design where private keys exist only on user's device
components: Secret keys never leave user's device

crypto.title: Quantum-Resistant Cryptography
crypto.description: Quantum Shield uses only NIST FIPS-certified algorithms. ECDSA/secp256k1 used in current blockchains is prohibited as it can be broken by quantum computers using Shor's algorithm.

dilithium.name: Dilithium-III
dilithium.description: Lattice-based digital signature. Used for users to sign unlock requests and Prover-operated L3 nodes to sign blocks.

sphincs.name: SPHINCS+-128s
sphincs.description: Hash-based digital signature. Used by Provers to sign unlock approvals. 7,856-byte signature size.

sha3.name: SHA3-256
sha3.description: Used for state root calculation, block hashing, Merkle trees, and all other hash operations.

prohibited.title: Prohibited (Quantum Vulnerable)
items: ECDSA / secp256k1 - Breakable by Shor's algorithm
SHA-256 / keccak256 - Attackable by Grover's algorithm

sequences.title: Processing Flows
sequences.description: Detailed sequences from lock to unlock. Each flow is executed by smart contracts and the L3 chain. Click Play to see the animated flow.

details.0: Send ETH + Dilithium public key hash to L3
details.1: Dilithium signature verification, nonce & expiry check
details.2: SHA3-256 hash of lock info, issue lock_id
details.3: ERC20 Transfer to L1Vault contract (~135K gas)
details.4: Lock complete, sync notification to L3 chain

details.3: HSM 2-of-3 internal approval, sign within 5 min
details.6: After timelock, ETH transferred to destination

emergency.title: Emergency Unlock
emergency.trigger: Automatically triggered when Provers don't respond for 72 hours

steps.1: Submit to L1 in emergency mode

details.0: No Prover signatures received, or VRF failure
details.1: No Prover sigs required, Emergency Mode flag set
details.2: MAX(0.5 ETH, 5% of amount) as collateral
details.4: If no challenge: bond returned + ETH transferred

dataFlow.sectionDesc: How data flows between User, L3 Aegis Chain, and L1 Ethereum. Each layer has specific responsibilities and uses different cryptographic algorithms.
dataFlow.title: System Data Flow

roles.title: Participant Roles
roles.description: Three key roles supporting the Quantum Shield ecosystem. Each monitors the others to ensure system security.

prover.title: Prover (L3 Node Operator)
prover.description: Core role operating L3 nodes and providing quantum-resistant signatures. Participates in BFT consensus while also providing SPHINCS+ signatures when selected by VRF. Manages private keys in HSM.
responsibilities: Operate L3 node and participate in BFT consensus
Generate SPHINCS+ signature when selected by VRF
Submit signature to L3 within 5 minutes
Manage private keys securely with HSM

observer.title: Observer (Monitor)
observer.description: Monitors fraudulent unlocks and earns rewards through challenges. Contributes to network security with 24/7 monitoring.
responsibilities: Detect fraud through Dilithium signature re-verification

user.title: User (Asset Holder)
user.description: General users protecting crypto assets with quantum resistance. Self-manage private keys and receive assets safely after 24-hour wait.
benefits: Protect assets from future quantum attacks
Only you control your private keys

specs.title: Key Parameters
specs.description: Important system configuration values. Timelocks are hardcoded in smart contracts and cannot be changed.

emergencyUnlock.label: Emergency Unlock
emergencyUnlock.description: Monitoring period after emergency path activation

challengeDefense.label: Challenge Defense
challengeDefense.description: Period for Prover to respond to challenge

sigDeadline.label: Signature Deadline
sigDeadline.description: Deadline for Provers to submit signatures

principles.title: Immutable Principles (Core Principles)
principles.description: These principles are hardcoded in smart contracts and defined as a 'constitution' that cannot be changed even by governance voting.

cp1.title: CP-1: Complete Quantum Resistance
cp1.description: All signatures and hashes are NIST FIPS compliant. Only Dilithium + SPHINCS+ + SHA3-256. ECDSA, SHA-256 prohibited.

cp2.title: CP-2: Self-Custody
cp2.description: User secret keys are never stored by the system. Client-side signing only. Asset control belongs to users.

cp3.title: CP-3: Timelock Existence
cp3.description: Timelocks cannot be set to 0. 24 hours (normal) / 7 days (emergency) are minimum guarantees. Ensures time for fraud detection.

cp4.title: CP-4: Slashing Existence
cp4.description: Slashing mechanism cannot be removed. N²×10% quadratic penalty strongly deters collusion.

cp5.title: CP-5: Transparency
cp5.description: All operations are verifiable on-chain. No secret computations exist. All state changes recorded on L3 chain.

rewards.title: Prover & Observer Rewards
rewards.description: Learn about reward models, staking requirements, and APY details for Provers and Observers in Token Hub.

cta.title: Start Using Quantum Shield
cta.description: Now that you understand the technical specifications, try using the apps.

glossary.title: Glossary
glossary.description: Explanations of technical terms used on this page.

pbft.definition: Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance. A consensus algorithm that works correctly even if 1/3 of nodes are malicious. Quantum Shield's Prover Network requires 3/4+ agreement.

vrf.definition: Verifiable Random Function. Generates tamper-proof random numbers that anyone can verify. Used to ensure fair Prover selection.

lattice.definition: Cryptography using mathematical 'lattice' structures. Considered resistant to quantum computers and forms the basis of Dilithium signatures.

hashBased.definition: Digital signatures using only hash functions. SPHINCS+ uses this method to provide the most conservative quantum resistance.

shor.definition: A quantum algorithm that can quickly factor integers. Could potentially break ECDSA used in Bitcoin and Ethereum.

grover.definition: A quantum algorithm that speeds up searching. It halves SHA-256's security, which is why we use SHA3-256.

timelock.definition: A mechanism preventing asset withdrawal until a certain time passes. During this waiting period, Observers can detect fraud and protect user assets.

slashing.definition: A penalty that confiscates the stake (collateral) of Provers who act maliciously. Makes collusion economically irrational.

hsm.definition: Hardware Security Module. Specialized hardware that physically protects private keys. Required for all Provers.

l3.definition: A decentralized network of L3 nodes operated by Provers. Each Prover operates an L3 node for consensus while also providing SPHINCS+ signatures when selected by VRF.

tooltips.pbft: Fault-tolerant consensus requiring 3/4+ Prover Network agreement
tooltips.vrf: Generates tamper-proof randomness for fair Prover selection
tooltips.lattice: Mathematical structure resistant to quantum computers
tooltips.hashBased: Most conservative signature method using only hash functions
tooltips.shor: Quantum algorithm that could break ECDSA
tooltips.grover: Quantum algorithm that halves SHA-256 security
tooltips.timelock: Waiting period for fraud detection. Cannot cancel during Observer monitoring
tooltips.slashing: Confiscates malicious Prover's stake. Penalty increases with collusion
tooltips.quadraticPenalty: For N colluders: N²×10% penalty. E.g., 3 colluders = 90% confiscated
tooltips.hsm: Specialized hardware for physical private key protection
tooltips.l3: Decentralized L3 network operated by Provers. Handles both BFT consensus and SPHINCS+ signing

why24Hours.title: Why 24-hour waiting?
why24Hours.content: The 24-hour waiting period allows Observers to detect fraudulent unlock requests. If there's a problem with signatures, a Challenge can be submitted during this time to protect your assets. This ensures your assets are safe even if a Prover acts maliciously.

why7Days.title: Why 7 days for emergency?
why7Days.content: Emergency unlocks execute without Prover signatures, so more careful monitoring is needed. The 7-day enhanced monitoring ensures the entire system is verified for fraud.

whyL3.title: Why L3 instead of L2?
whyL3.content: Existing L2s are ECDSA-based and quantum-vulnerable. By building a Prover-operated L3 network, we achieve a fully quantum-resistant design integrating BFT consensus and SPHINCS+ signatures.
